title: Pulse — SaaS Trial Signup Landing Page
description: An original free-trial signup landing page sample for a fictional product-analytics SaaS — built to test a single-CTA, no-card-required conversion pattern.
longDescription: Pulse is a self-initiated landing-page sample, not a real product. I built it to apply the same conversion-focused layout discipline I use for client PPC pages: one clear CTA above the fold, friction-reducing copy ('no credit card required'), and a feature grid that answers objections instead of just listing capabilities.
highlights: Single repeated CTA (hero + footer) instead of a multi-link nav, to avoid splitting attention
"No credit card required" placed directly under the CTA to pre-empt the #1 signup objection
Feature grid framed around outcomes (what you see/avoid) rather than feature names alone

caseStudy.problem: Decision fatigue is the real killer on SaaS trial pages. Too many nav links, feature lists that don't say anything concrete, and a credit-card ask nobody mentions until checkout — by the time a visitor half-decides to try the product, they've already found three reasons to hesitate.
caseStudy.approach: So I cut it down to one path. Headline, one email field, one CTA — repeated once more at the bottom for anyone who scrolls first. No navbar. Nothing on the page is allowed to exist unless it's either building trust in the next ten seconds or pushing toward the form.
decisions: No navigation at all. A PPC visitor already had a reason to be here — giving them four more places to click just splits that intent.
'No credit card required · 14-day trial · Cancel anytime' sits directly under the button, not in a footnote, because that's the exact moment someone hesitates.
Three feature cards instead of a longer list, each written as a benefit rather than a spec sheet — PPC traffic skims, it doesn't read.
caseStudy.outcome: // TODO: No real traffic — this is a self-initiated sample, not a deployed campaign. Conversion data not available.
